content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 15, 1976

Frank Zolin, Executive Officer
The Superior Court
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Dear Mr. Zolin:

Thank you for your letter of June 29, 1976 requesting advice about the reporting requirements for judges filing Statements of Economic Interests. With respect to your first and second questions, incumbent judges who are reelected in 1976 must file Form 721 no later than February 2, 1977 and each statement must cover the period from May 5, 1976 to January 2, 1977. Since incumbent judges who are reelected are not deemed to be leaving office or assuming a new office, they need not file a Form 720 pursuant to the procedures set forth in Government Code Sections 87202 and 87204. 1/ It should be emphasized, however, that your questions and this advice are limited to incumbent judges who seek reelection and a different filing schedule may exist for incumbent judges not seeking reelection and for noninincumbents.

In answer to your third question concerning the reporting requirements of judges' spouses, the spouse of a filer has no reporting obligation of his or her own. However, in filing a Statement of Economic Interests, the filer is required to list certain economic interests of his (her) spouse and dependent children. Hopefully, the following comments will clarify these responsibilities.

"Investments," as defined in Section 82034, and "Interests in Real Property," as defined in Section 82033, must be reported in the manner described in Section 87206. Furthermore, Government Code Section 82029 provides:

1/ All statutory references will be to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Frank Zolin
September 13, 1976
Page Two

"Immediate family" means the spouse
and dependent children. Whenever dis-
closure of investments or interests in
real property is required by this title,
investments and interest in real property
of members of the immediate family shall
also be disclosed.

Thus, where a member of a judge's immediate family has eco-
nomic interests of the type described in Sections 82033 and
82034, they should be reported in precisely the same manner
as if they were held by the filer.

Slightly different considerations exist with re-
spect to reporting income. "Income" is defined in Section
82030, which provides:

(a) "Income" means, except as pro-
vided in subsection (b), income of any
nature from any source, including but not
limited to any salary, wage, advance, pay-
ment, dividend, interest, rent, capital
gain, return of capital, gift, including
any gift of food or beverage, loan, for-
giveness or payment of indebtedness, dis-
count in the price of anything of value
unless the discount is available to mem-
bers of the public without regard to of-
ficial status, rebate, reimbursement for
expenses, per diem, or contribution to
an insurance or pension program paid by
any person other than an employer, and
including any community property interest
in income of a spouse. Income of an in-
dividual also includes a pro rata share
of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse
owns, directly, indirectly or benefi-
cially, a ten percent interest or
greater.

(b) "Income" does not include:
(1) Campaign contributions required
to be reported under Chapter 4 of this
title;
(2) Salary and reimbursement for
expenses or per diem received from a
state or local government agency and
reimbursement for travel expenses and
per diem received from a bona fide ed-
ucational, academic or charitable
organization;

Frank Zolin
September 15, 1976
Page Three


     (3) Gifts of informational material,
such as books, pamphlets, reports, cal-
endars or periodicals;
     (4) Gifts which are not used and
which, within thirty days after receipt,
are returned to the donor or delivered
to a charitable organization without
being claimed as a charitable contribu-
tion for tax purposes;
     (5) Gifts from an individual's spouse,
child, parent, grandparent, grandchild,
brother, sister, parent-in-law, brother-
in-law, sister-in-law, aunt, uncle, or
first cousin or the spouse of any such
person, provided that a gift from any
such person shall be considered income
if the donor is acting as an agent or
intermediary for any person not covered
by this paragraph;
     (6) Any devise or inheritance;
     (7) Interest, dividends or pre-
miums on a time or demand deposit in a
financial institution, shares in a credit
union or any insurance policy, payments
received under any insurance policy, or
any bond or other debt instrument issued
by any government or government agency;
     (8) Dividends, interest or any other
return on a security which is registered
with the Securities and Exchange Commis-
sion of the United States Government.

     A filer therefore must report, in the manner prescribed in
Section 87207, all income received directly by the filer.
In addition, a filer must report his community property
interest in income received by the filer's spouse and, in
the case of income from a business entity in which the spouse
has a ten percent interest or greater, his interest in the
spouse's pro rata share of such income.

     In light of the fact that it is the community
property interest in a spouse's income which must be dis-
closed, the threshold level for triggering reporting re-
quirements based on a spouse's income is in effect doubled.
Thus, the threshold level is $500 (instead of $250) in the
case of most sources of income to the spouse; $50 with respect

Frank Zolin
September 15, 1976
Page Four

to gifts to the spouse,2/ and $2000 in fees and $20,000 in
gross receipts with respect to the disclosure of the identity of persons providing income to business entities in
which the spouse has a ten percent interest or greater. See
Section 872(b).

This analysis then leads us into the second half
of your third question concerning spouses who are practicing
attorneys and are reluctant to disclose the identity of
their clients to the filer. Initially, it will be helpful
to consider what type of disclosure generally would be made
by a filer whose spouse is a practicing attorney. If the
spouse owns less than a ten percent interest in a law firm,
the law firm, and only the law firm, is the "source of income" to the filer to the extent of the filer's community
property interest in the income the spouse receives from the
law firm. Accordingly, pursuant to Section 87207, the filer
would list only the law firm as a source of income and would
not have to list any individual clients of the firm on his
Statement of Economic Interests.

If, on the other hand, a spouse who is a practicing attorney owns directly, indirectly or beneficially a ten
percent interest or greater in a law firm, then income of
the filer includes his interest in the spouse's pro rata
share of the income of the firm. Section 82030, and clients
of the firm become "sources of income" to the filer. However,
only clients who pay fees to the business entity of such
magnitude as to result in the filer's pro rata share being
equal to or greater than $1000 need be specifically listed
by the filer. In light of our earlier analysis, this means
that the spouse's pro rata share of the fees to the law firm
must equal at least $2000 in order for any reporting requirement concerning an individual client of the firm to attach
to the filer. The filer, in addition, will still report the
firm as a "source of income." See Form 721.

Given this reporting requirement, the question
becomes what is the filer's responsibility in these matters
when dealing with a "reluctant spouse." Under the Act, each

2/ It should be noted that gifts made to the
spouse alone which are not likely to benefit the filer and,
in fact, do not benefit the filer, are not reportable. See
Opinion requested by Kenneth Cory, 2 FPPC Opinions 48 (No.
75-94-A, April 22, 1976), which establishes criteria for
when a gift is made to the spouse alone.

Frank Zolin
September 15, 1976
Page Five

Filer is required to use reasonable diligence in the com-
pletion of all statements and reports. Section 81004.
Accordingly, if the spouse informs the filer or if the filer
knows of her reason to know of the identity of the spouse's
clients, the filer must disclose the required information
even if the spouse would prefer that the filer not do so.
If, however, the filer does not know or have reason to know
the identity of such clients and if the filer's spouse refuses
to provide the filer with this information, the Act does not
require the filer to take additional action to determine the
identity of the clients.

Finally, you have asked how stock transactions
must be reported. Investments (which include common or
preferred stock, options, and other ownership interests)
generally are reported in the manner prescribed in Section
87200. See also, Forms 720 and 721 and the explanatory
materials. In addition, capital gains realized on the sale
of stock are income, Section 82030(a), and therefore must be
reported pursuant to Section 87207. However, the Commission
recently adopted a regulation which declares that "if by the
exercise of reasonable diligence the filer does not know or
have reason to know the identity of the purchaser of a capital
asset, the capital gain need not be reported." 2 Cal.Adm.
Code Section 18729. The effect of this regulation is to
obviate the need to report typical stock transactions con-
ducted through a stock exchange where the identity of the
purchaser is not known.

I hope the foregoing is responsive to your ques-
tions and I apologize for the delay in responding to your
letter. If you have any further questions, please contact
Ken Goshorn or Dawn Wiser of the Commission's Conflict of
Interest Division at (916) 322-6444. They will be happy to
assist in the completion of Statements of Economic Interests
or the resolution of any other problems which you or the
judges may encounter.

Sincerely,

Kenneth Finney
Chief
Legal Division

KF:plh

The Superior Court
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012
FRANK S. ZOLIN, EXECUTIVE OFFICER

TELEPHONE
(213) 974-5401

June 29, 1976

Mr. John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator
Fair Political Practices Commission
107 South Broadway
Room 2140-A, State Building
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Dear John:

Thank you for forwarding the memorandums regarding Financial
Disclosure Reporting and additional copies of Forms 720 and
721.

As you know, everyone is greatly confused regarding the
reporting requirements of Superior Court Judges. The most
common questions to date have been as follows:

1. Exactly what date do incumbent judges
   running for election in 1976 have to
   file Form 721?

2. Exactly what date do incumbent judges
   running for election in 1976 have to
   file Form 720?

3. What are the reporting requirements of
   judges' spouses, and what happens if a
   judge's spouse is a practicing attorney
   and does not wish to disclose his source
   of income?

4. What is the actual requirement for
   reporting stock transactions. Since
   several judges trade a great number
   of stocks each year, are they required
   to list each and every transaction,
   including name, address, location of
   firms, etc. (As you can imagine this

Mr. John C. Greenwood
June 29, 1976
Page Two

                    is a tedious and difficult task if one
                    has a great deal of stock--at least
                    that's what I've heard--not knowing
                    from personal experience.)

     It would be greatly appreciated if you could answer these
questions at your earliest convenience. I am sure you are
familiar with the typical questions asked by individuals
required to complete these forms. Please add whatever other
information you think might be helpful to the judges of the
L.A. Superior Court. There seems to be some confusion
regarding the reporting of gifts and any clarification you
can provide will be helpful.

     Your cooperation and interest in assisting the judges of the
Court is greatly appreciated.

     Sincerely,

     [signature]
     Frank Zolin
     Executive Officer

     FZ:ch

sections.conclusion: Incumbent judges reelected in 1975 must file Form 721 no later than February 2, 1977, covering the period from May 5, 1975 to January 2, 1977, and need not file Form 720. Judges' spouses have no independent reporting obligation but their economic interests must be reported by the judge filer. When a spouse practices law and owns less than 10% of a law firm, only the law firm is listed as income source; if the spouse owns 10% or greater, individual clients become reportable sources if fees reach the $1000 threshold. The filer must use reasonable diligence but is not required to take additional action if the spouse refuses to provide client information. Stock transactions and capital gains must be reported, but the identity of purchasers in typical stock exchange transactions need not be disclosed if the filer has exercised reasonable diligence.
sections.question_synthetic: Frank Zolin requests clarification on the reporting requirements for judges filing Statements of Economic Interests, specifically regarding filing deadlines for incumbent judges, spousal reporting obligations (particularly when spouses are practicing attorneys unwilling to disclose client information), and stock transaction disclosure requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Incumbent judges reelected in 1975 must file Form 721 by February 2, 1977 without filing Form 720. While judges' spouses have no independent reporting obligation, the judge must report their economic interests using reasonable diligence; however, when a spouse is a reluctant attorney, the judge need not take additional action to discover client identities beyond what the spouse discloses. Stock transactions must be reported as investments and capital gains as income, but purchaser identities from typical stock exchange transactions need not be disclosed.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated June 29, 1976 from Frank Zolin) and the FPPC response (dated September 15, 1976 from Kenneth Finney). OCR quality is moderate with some transcription artifacts but content is substantially legible. Question section reconstructed from incoming letter as no separate QUESTION header in response letter; conclusion is synthesized from the substantive analysis provided.

QUESTION: s at your earliest convenience.
I am sure you are
familiar with the typical questions asked by individuals
required to complete these forms.
Please add whatever other
information you think night he helpful to the judges cf the
L. A. Superior Court.
There seems to be some confusion
regarding the reporting uf gifts and any clarification you
can provide will be helpful.
Your cooperation and interest in assisting the judges of the
Court is greatly appreciated.

CONCLUSION: Incumbent judges reelected in 1975 must file Form 721 no later than February 2, 1977, covering the period from May 5, 1975 to January 2, 1977, and need not file Form 720. Judges' spouses have no independent reporting obligation but their economic interests must be reported by the judge filer. When a spouse practices law and owns less than 10% of a law firm, only the law firm is listed as income source; if the spouse owns 10% or greater, individual clients become reportable sources if fees reach the $1000 threshold. The filer must use reasonable diligence but is not required to take additional action if the spouse refuses to provide client information. Stock transactions and capital gains must be reported, but the identity of purchasers in typical stock exchange transactions need not be disclosed if the filer has exercised reasonable diligence.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides guidance to Superior Court judges on financial disclosure reporting requirements, addressing filing deadlines, spousal reporting obligations, attorney-spouse client confidentiality issues, and stock transaction reporting through a detailed advice letter responding to four specific questions from the Los Angeles Superior Court Executive Officer.
